title: Financial _Options_, Valuation, and Real Options
summary: Options are financial contracts giving their holder the right — but not the obligation — to buy or sell an asset at a pre-agreed price on or before a specified date. This section develops the complete toolkit for understanding, pricing, and applying options in a corporate-finance context: from payoff diagrams and put–call parity (Chapter 20), through the binomial model and Black–Scholes formula (Chapter 21), to the application of option-pricing logic to real investment decisions (Chapter 22). Together these chapters show why the Law of One Price, not investor risk preferences, is sufficient to value derivative securities, and how the same logic reveals that equity is a call option on firm assets — an insight with deep implications for capital structure, agency costs, and investment timing.

subsections.title: Risk and Return of an Option
The Option to Delay: Investment as a Call Option

detailed_notes:
# Chapter 20 — Financial Options

## 20.1 Option Basics

A **financial option** is a contract that gives its owner the right — but not the obligation — to purchase or sell an asset at a fixed price at some future date. Because the holder is never forced to act, options have non-negative payoffs at expiration. Two fundamental types exist, and every other option strategy is built from them.

### Types of Options

**Definition:** A **call option** gives its holder the right to *buy* the underlying asset at the agreed price on or before the expiration date. A **put option** gives its holder the right to *sell* the underlying asset.

Key contract terms:
- **Strike price (exercise price)** $K$: the price at which the holder may buy (call) or sell (put) the asset.
- **Expiration date**: the last date on which the option may be exercised.
- **American option**: may be exercised on *any* date up to and including the expiration date.
- **European option**: may be exercised *only* on the expiration date.
- **Option writer (seller)**: takes the short side; has an *obligation* to fulfil the contract if exercised. Receives the **option premium** upfront.
- **Option holder (buyer)**: has the long position; pays the premium.

### Moneyness

At any date before expiration, the option's **moneyness** describes the relationship between the stock price $S$ and the strike price $K$:

| Label | Call condition | Put condition |
|---|---|---|
| In-the-money | $S > K$ | $S < K$ |
| At-the-money | $S = K$ | $S = K$ |
| Out-of-the-money | $S < K$ | $S > K$ |

When $S$ and $K$ are *very* far apart the option is *deep* in- or out-of-the-money.

**Key insight:** Options on stocks trade on exchanges (e.g. the CBOE). Each listed equity option contract covers 100 shares; prices are quoted per share. Open interest is the total number of outstanding contracts of a given option.

---

## 20.2 Option Payoffs at Expiration

Because the Law of One Price values any security by its future cash flows, we begin by mapping out option payoffs precisely.

### Long Call Payoff

Let $S$ be the stock price at expiration and $K$ the strike price. The call holder exercises only when $S > K$ (capturing the difference) and does nothing otherwise. The value of the call at expiration is therefore

$$C = \max(S - K,\; 0) \tag{20.1}$$

**Worked Example (call payoff):** Strike $K = \$20$.

| $S$ | $C = \max(S-20,0)$ |
|---|---|
| \$10 | \$0 |
| \$20 | \$0 |
| \$22 | \$2 |
| \$30 | \$10 |
| \$40 | \$20 |

The payoff diagram is a hockey-stick shape: flat at zero for $S \leq K$, then rising dollar-for-dollar with $S$.

### Long Put Payoff

The put holder exercises only when $S < K$ (selling at $K$ while the market only offers $S$):

$$P = \max(K - S,\; 0) \tag{20.2}$$

The payoff diagram slopes down from $K$ (when $S=0$) to zero at $S = K$, then stays flat at zero.

### Short Positions

The option *writer* receives the mirror image of the holder's payoff. A short call pays $-\max(S-K,0)$ (losses mount without bound as $S$ rises); a short put pays $-\max(K-S,0)$ (maximum loss $= K$ when $S = 0$).

### Profit vs. Payoff

Payoff diagrams ignore the premium paid. **Profit** accounts for the initial cost:

$$\text{Profit}_{\text{call}} = \max(S-K,0) - C_0 \times (1+r_f)^{T}$$

where $C_0$ is the call premium and $T$ is time to expiration. The break-even stock price for a long call is $K + C_0(1+r_f)^T$. The maximum loss on a purchased option is always 100% of the premium paid.

### Option Combinations

**Straddle:** long call + long put, same $K$ and expiration. Pays off when $S$ is far from $K$ in either direction. Used when large volatility is expected but direction is uncertain.

**Strangle:** long call (strike $K_C$) + long put (strike $K_P$), same expiration but $K_C > K_P$. Cheaper than a straddle; requires a larger move to profit.

**Butterfly spread:** long one call at $K_1$, long one call at $K_3$, short two calls at $K_2 = (K_1+K_3)/2$. Pays off when $S$ is *near* the middle strike; profits are capped.

**Protective put / Portfolio insurance:** Owning a stock and buying a put on it ensures the total value never falls below $K$. This is equivalent (by put–call parity, Section 20.3) to holding a zero-coupon bond with face value $K$ plus a call option.

---

## 20.3 Put-Call Parity

Portfolio insurance can be constructed in two equivalent ways:
1. Buy the stock and buy a European put with strike $K$.
2. Buy a zero-coupon bond with face value $K$ (cost $= PV(K)$) and buy a European call with strike $K$.

Both positions have identical payoffs at expiration: they deliver $\max(S_T, K)$. By the **Law of One Price** they must therefore have the same price today.

$$S + P = PV(K) + C \tag{20.3}$$

Rearranging gives the most useful form — the **put–call parity** formula:

$$\boxed{C = P + S - PV(K)} \tag{20.3}$$

For a **dividend-paying** stock the holder of the stock (but not the call) also receives dividends $Div$ before expiration. Adjusting:

$$C = P + S - PV(Div) - PV(K) \tag{20.4}$$

**Key insight:** Put–call parity holds for European options. It can be used to (i) price a call given a put (or vice versa), (ii) detect arbitrage, or (iii) extract implied dividends from market prices.

**Worked Example (using put–call parity):** HAL stock trades at $S = \$18$. A one-year European put with $K = \$20$ costs $P = \$3.50$. Risk-free rate $r_f = 6\%$. What is the fair call price?

$$PV(K) = \frac{20}{1.06} = \$18.868$$

$$C = P + S - PV(K) = 3.50 + 18 - 18.868 = \$2.632$$

Any call price above \$2.632 would allow an arbitrage by selling the call and buying the replicating portfolio of put + stock − bond.

---

## 20.4 Factors Affecting Option Prices

Before we can derive a full pricing formula, we can establish **arbitrage bounds** and identify the key drivers of option value.

### Arbitrage Bounds

- **An American option ≥ its European counterpart** (it has all the same rights plus the early-exercise privilege).
- **Put value ≤ $K$** (the put is worth at most the strike price, achieved only if the stock is worthless).
- **Call value ≤ $S$** (a zero-strike call is worth the stock itself).
- **American option ≥ its intrinsic value** (otherwise, immediate exercise yields an arbitrage).

**Definition:** The **intrinsic value** of an option is its value if exercised immediately — the amount by which it is in the money, or zero. The **time value** is the difference between the current option price and its intrinsic value:

$$\text{Time value} = \text{Option price} - \text{Intrinsic value}$$

American options always have non-negative time value.

### Effect of the Strike Price

For calls: lower $K$ ⟹ higher $C$ (cheaper to buy the stock). For puts: higher $K$ ⟹ higher $P$ (better selling price). Holding expiration constant, the spread between otherwise identical options with different strikes reflects the probability distribution of future stock prices.

### Effect of Volatility

**Key insight:** Option value **increases with volatility** $\sigma$. Intuitively, higher volatility raises the probability of very high stock prices (valuable for a call) *and* very low stock prices (valuable for a put), but the holder's downside is capped at zero in either case. The asymmetric payoff means higher volatility is always beneficial to the option holder.

**Worked Example (volatility and option value):** Two European calls, $K = \$50$, expire tomorrow. Stock A is certain to be \$50 (zero volatility); its call is worth \$0. Stock B will be \$60 or \$40 with equal probability (expected value still \$50); its call pays \$10 with 50% probability, so it has positive value today. Volatility creates option value even when expected payoffs are identical.

---

## 20.5 Exercising Options Early

### Non-Dividend-Paying Stocks

Using put–call parity for a non-dividend-paying stock and writing $PV(K) = K - \text{dis}(K)$ where $\text{dis}(K)$ is the discount on the bond:

$$C = \underbrace{(S-K)}_{\text{intrinsic value}} + \underbrace{\text{dis}(K) + P}_{\text{time value}} \tag{20.5}$$

Both components of the time value are **positive** as long as interest rates and put prices are positive. Therefore a European call on a non-dividend-paying stock always exceeds its intrinsic value, and exercising early sacrifices that time value. **It is never optimal to exercise an American call on a non-dividend-paying stock early**; consequently, its price equals its European counterpart.

For **American puts** on non-dividend-paying stocks, the time value can be negative (the opportunity cost of not receiving $K$ today is large when the put is deep in-the-money). Early exercise can therefore be optimal for deep in-the-money puts.

### Dividend-Paying Stocks

When dividends $PV(Div)$ are large, the time value of a call can turn negative:

$$C = (S-K) + \text{dis}(K) + P - PV(Div) \tag{20.7}$$

If $PV(Div)$ exceeds $\text{dis}(K) + P$, the call's time value is negative and an American call may be worth more than its European counterpart — early exercise just before the ex-dividend date captures the dividend. **Early exercise is optimal for sufficiently deep in-the-money calls on dividend-paying stocks just before the ex-dividend date.**

---

## 20.6 Options and Corporate Finance

Option theory provides a powerful lens for interpreting the capital structure of any levered firm.

### Equity as a Call Option

Consider a firm that will be liquidated in one period. Let $A$ be the value of the firm's assets and $D$ be the face value of its debt.

- If $A > D$: equity holders receive $A - D > 0$ after repaying debt.
- If $A \leq D$: the firm defaults; equity holders receive $0$.

This is precisely the payoff of a **call option** on the firm's assets with strike price equal to the required debt payment:

$$E = \max(A - D,\; 0)$$

### Debt as an Option Portfolio

Debt holders receive $\min(A, D)$ — the full promised payment if the firm is solvent, or the asset value in default. This can be decomposed as:

$$\text{Risky debt} = \text{Risk-free debt} - \text{Put option on firm assets} \tag{20.9}$$

The put option with strike $D$ is called a **credit default swap (CDS)**: buying it converts risky debt into risk-free debt.

### Agency Conflicts through the Options Lens

- **Asset substitution:** Because equity is a call option, its value increases with asset volatility. Equity holders may therefore prefer riskier investments even at the expense of debt holders (who are short the put).
- **Debt overhang:** Because $\Delta < 1$ for a call, equity holders capture only a fraction of each dollar invested, reducing their incentive to invest in positive-NPV projects.

Both agency costs can be quantified using option-pricing methods — a preview of the tools derived in Chapter 21.

---

# Chapter 21 — Option Valuation

## 21.1 The Binomial Option Pricing Model

The key insight of Black, Scholes, and Merton is that option payoffs can be replicated exactly by continuously trading the underlying stock and a risk-free bond. The **Binomial Option Pricing Model** makes this concrete by assuming that in each period the stock can move to only two possible prices.

### A Two-State Single-Period Model

Assume:
- Current stock price $S = \$50$.
- In one period: either $S_u = \$60$ (up state) or $S_d = \$40$ (down state).
- One-period risk-free rate $r_f = 6\%$.
- European call, strike $K = \$50$, expires in one period.

The option payoffs are $C_u = \max(60-50,0) = \$10$ and $C_d = \max(40-50,0) = \$0$.

We construct a **replicating portfolio** of $\Delta$ shares and $B$ dollars invested in a risk-free bond such that the portfolio value matches the option in every state:

$$60\Delta + 1.06B = 10 \quad (\text{up state})$$
$$40\Delta + 1.06B = 0 \quad (\text{down state})$$

Subtracting the second equation from the first:

$$20\Delta = 10 \implies \Delta = 0.5$$

Substituting back: $B = -10/(1.06 \times 2) = -\$18.87$ (borrow \$18.87).

By the **Law of One Price**, the call price today equals the cost of the replicating portfolio:

$$C = S\Delta + B = 50(0.5) - 18.87 = \$6.13 \tag{21.3}$$

**Note:** We did not use the probability of the up or down state anywhere — the Law of One Price alone is sufficient.

### The General Binomial Pricing Formula

For a general stock with current price $S$, up price $S_u$, down price $S_d$, and option values $C_u$ and $C_d$:

$$\Delta = \frac{C_u - C_d}{S_u - S_d} \qquad B = \frac{C_d - S_d \Delta}{1 + r_f} \tag{21.5}$$

$$C = S\Delta + B \tag{21.6}$$

$\Delta$ is the **option delta** — the number of shares in the replicating portfolio — and equals the slope of the option payoff as a function of the stock price. $B < 0$ for calls (borrowing) and $B > 0$ for puts (lending).

**Worked Example (put option via binomial):** Stock at \$60, goes up 20% to \$72 or down 10% to \$54; $r_f = 3\%$; European put $K = \$60$.

$C_u = \max(60-72,0) = 0$, $C_d = \max(60-54,0) = 6$.

$$\Delta = \frac{0 - 6}{72 - 54} = -\frac{1}{3} \qquad B = \frac{6 - 54(-1/3)}{1.03} = \$23.30$$

$$P = 60(-1/3) + 23.30 = \$3.30$$

### A Multiperiod Model

With multiple periods, we work **backward** through the binomial tree — a procedure called **backward induction**:

1. At the final date, compute intrinsic values at every node.
2. At each earlier node, apply the single-period formula (Eqs. 21.5–21.6) to compute the option value and replicating portfolio.
3. As the stock price changes, the portfolio must be **rebalanced** — this is a **dynamic trading strategy**.

**Worked Example (two-period call):** Stock starts at \$40; goes to \$50 or \$30 each period; $r_f = 6\%$; call $K = \$50$.

At $t=2$: $C_{uu} = 10$, $C_{ud} = C_{du} = C_{dd} = 0$.

At $t=1$, up node ($S=50$): same as one-period example ⟹ $C_u = \$6.13$.
At $t=1$, down node ($S=30$): both future values are zero ⟹ $C_d = \$0$.

At $t=0$:
$$\Delta = \frac{6.13 - 0}{50 - 30} = 0.3065 \qquad B = \frac{0 - 30(0.3065)}{1.06} = -\$8.67$$
$$C = 40(0.3065) - 8.67 = \$3.59$$

### Making the Model Realistic

Binary moves are unrealistic over a full year, but become a good approximation when the period length is very short. By letting each period shrink to zero and the number of periods grow to infinity, the binomial model converges to the **Black-Scholes formula** — a continuous-time, closed-form result.

---

## 21.2 The Black-Scholes Option Pricing Model

The Black–Scholes model prices European options by assuming the stock price evolves continuously and log-normally. It requires only five inputs — no estimate of the expected return on the stock is needed.

### The Black-Scholes Formula for Calls

**Proposition (Black-Scholes Call Price):** The value of a European call on a non-dividend-paying stock is

$$C = S \times N(d_1) - PV(K) \times N(d_2) \tag{21.7}$$

where $PV(K) = K/(1+r_f)^T$ is the present value of the strike price, $N(d)$ is the **cumulative standard normal distribution** (the probability that a standard normal random variable is less than $d$), and

$$d_1 = \frac{\ln[S/PV(K)]}{\sigma\sqrt{T}} + \frac{\sigma\sqrt{T}}{2}, \qquad d_2 = d_1 - \sigma\sqrt{T} \tag{21.8}$$

Here $\sigma$ is the annualised volatility of the stock return and $T$ is years to expiration.

**Five inputs:** current stock price $S$, strike price $K$, time to expiration $T$, risk-free rate $r_f$, and volatility $\sigma$. Crucially, the expected return of the stock is **not** an input.

**Worked Example (CLW Inc.):** $S = \$24$, $K = \$30$, $T = 0.5$ year, $\sigma = 45\%$, $r_f = 5\%$.

$$PV(K) = \frac{30}{1.05^{0.5}} = \$29.277$$

$$d_1 = \frac{\ln(24/29.277)}{0.45\sqrt{0.5}} + \frac{0.45\sqrt{0.5}}{2} = -0.4658$$

$$d_2 = -0.4658 - 0.45\sqrt{0.5} = -0.7840$$

$$N(d_1) = N(-0.4658) \approx 0.3207, \qquad N(d_2) = N(-0.7840) \approx 0.2165$$

$$C = 24(0.3207) - 29.277(0.2165) = \$7.697 - \$6.338 \approx \$1.36$$

### The Black-Scholes Formula for European Puts

Applying put–call parity to the Black-Scholes call formula gives

$$P = PV(K)[1 - N(d_2)] - S[1 - N(d_1)] \tag{21.9}$$

### Dividend-Paying Stocks

A European call does not receive dividends paid before expiration. Replace $S$ with

$$S^* = S - PV(Div) \tag{21.10}$$

or, if the stock pays a compounded dividend yield $q$ before expiration,

$$S^* = \frac{S}{1+q} \tag{21.11}$$

Then apply the standard Black-Scholes formula with $S^*$ in place of $S$.

### The Black-Scholes Replicating Portfolio

Comparing $C = S\Delta + B$ to Eq. 21.7 gives the explicit Black-Scholes replicating portfolio for a **call**:

$$\Delta = N(d_1), \qquad B = -PV(K)\,N(d_2) \tag{21.12}$$

For a **European put**:

$$\Delta = -[1 - N(d_1)], \qquad B = PV(K)[1-N(d_2)] \tag{21.13}$$

For calls, $0 < \Delta \leq 1$ (long stock, short bond — levered position). For puts, $-1 \leq \Delta < 0$ (short stock, long bond).

### Implied Volatility

**Definition:** **Implied volatility** is the value of $\sigma$ that, when inserted into the Black-Scholes formula, reproduces the market price of a traded option. It is obtained by inverting the formula numerically (e.g. via Excel's Solver).

Implied volatility is widely used to (i) cross-check option prices across strikes and maturities, (ii) forecast near-term market uncertainty, and (iii) price exotic or illiquid options. The CBOE **VIX Index** aggregates one-month implied volatility from S&P 500 options and is sometimes called the "fear index" — it spikes dramatically during market crises.

---

## 21.3 Risk-Neutral Probabilities

The binomial and Black-Scholes models price options without knowing investor risk preferences or the actual probability of each state. This suggests a clever alternative technique.

**Definition:** **Risk-neutral probabilities** are the hypothetical probabilities under which every asset earns the risk-free rate as its expected return. They are also called *state prices*, *state-contingent prices*, or *martingale prices*.

In a binomial tree, the risk-neutral probability $p$ that the stock price increases satisfies:

$$\frac{p\,S_u + (1-p)\,S_d}{S} - 1 = r_f$$

Solving:

$$p = \frac{(1+r_f)S - S_d}{S_u - S_d} \tag{21.16}$$

**Key insight:** Using risk-neutral probabilities $p$ and $(1-p)$, the price of any derivative security equals the **expected payoff under risk-neutral probabilities, discounted at the risk-free rate**. No assumption about actual investor risk preferences is needed — the current stock price already embeds that information.

**Worked Example:** From our single-period example: $S = 50$, $S_u = 60$, $S_d = 40$, $r_f = 6\%$.

$$p = \frac{1.06 \times 50 - 40}{60 - 40} = \frac{13}{20} = 65\%$$

Call payoff: $C_u = 10$ with probability 65%, $C_d = 0$ with probability 35%:

$$C = \frac{0.65 \times 10 + 0.35 \times 0}{1.06} = \$6.13$$

Identical to the replicating-portfolio answer — as it must be.

**Remark:** The risk-neutral probability (65%) differs from the true probability of an up move (say, 75%). The difference accounts for the risk premium. Risk-neutral probabilities *underweight* good states and *overweight* bad states relative to reality, which is why discounting at the risk-free rate is sufficient.

---

## 21.4 Risk and Return of an Option

Because an option's value is derived from the replicating portfolio, its risk characteristics are also inherited from that portfolio.

### Option Beta

The beta of a portfolio is the value-weighted average beta of its components. Since the bond has $\beta_B = 0$:

$$\boxed{\beta_{\text{option}} = \frac{S\Delta}{S\Delta + B}\,\beta_S} \tag{21.17}$$

The ratio $S\Delta/(S\Delta+B)$ is the **leverage ratio** of the option.

- For a **call** on a positive-beta stock: $\Delta > 0$, $B < 0$ ⟹ $\beta_{\text{call}} > \beta_S > 0$. Calls amplify stock risk.
- For a **put** on a positive-beta stock: $\Delta < 0$, $B > 0$ ⟹ $\beta_{\text{put}} < 0$. Puts hedge stock risk.
- Out-of-the-money options have *higher-magnitude* leverage ratios and therefore more extreme betas.

**Worked Example (JetBlue):** Call: $S = \$5.03$, $C = \$0.50$, $N(d_1) = 0.417$, $\beta_S = 0.85$.

$$\beta_{\text{call}} = \frac{5.03 \times 0.417}{0.50} \times 0.85 = \frac{2.097}{0.50} \times 0.85 \approx 3.57$$

Put: $P = \$0.87$, $1-N(d_1) = 0.401$:

$$\beta_{\text{put}} = \frac{-5.03 \times 0.401}{0.87} \times 0.85 \approx -1.97$$

The call is roughly four times riskier than the stock; the put has negative expected return (it is held as insurance).

---

## 21.5 Corporate Applications of Option Pricing

### Beta of Risky Debt

When debt is risky, the standard leverage formula $\beta_E = \beta_U(1 + D/E)$ overstates equity beta because it assumes $\beta_D = 0$. Using the option interpretation of equity as a call on firm assets $A = E + D$ with delta $\Delta$:

$$\beta_E = \frac{A\Delta}{E}\,\beta_U = \Delta\!\left(1 + \frac{D}{E}\right)\!\beta_U \tag{21.19}$$

$$\beta_D = (1-\Delta)\left(1 + \frac{E}{D}\right)\!\beta_U \tag{21.20}$$

When debt is risk-free, $\Delta = 1$ and these reduce to the familiar formulas. As leverage increases and debt becomes riskier, $\Delta < 1$, the beta of debt rises above zero, and the beta of equity grows more slowly than the simple formula predicts.

### Agency Costs Quantified

**Asset substitution:** Equity value increases with firm asset volatility (call option vega > 0). Equity holders may therefore support risky negative-NPV projects that transfer value from debt holders.

**Debt overhang:** Because $\Delta < 1$, equity holders capture only $\Delta$ of each dollar increase in firm asset value from new investment. They benefit from a positive-NPV project only if:

$$\frac{\text{NPV}}{I} \geq \frac{(1-\Delta)}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\beta_D D}{\beta_E E}$$

verifying the underinvestment condition derived from agency theory in Chapter 16.

**Caveats:** The Black-Scholes formula assumes constant volatility and continuous trading. For employee stock options (ESOs), early exercise, forfeitures, and the inability of employees to hedge make the formula inappropriate; it overstates the option's value to the employee and can overstate the cost to the firm by as much as 40%.

---

# Chapter 22 — Real Options

## 22.1 Real Versus Financial Options

A **real option** is the right — but not the obligation — to make a particular business decision (such as a capital investment) after new information arrives. Unlike financial options, the underlying asset is typically not traded in a competitive market, but the same valuation principles apply: the presence of a real option adds value, especially in uncertain environments.

**Definition:** A **real option** differs from a financial option in that the "underlying asset" is a business decision or physical asset rather than a traded security. Nevertheless, because payoffs conditional on future states can be modelled, the Law of One Price and option-pricing tools remain applicable.

---

## 22.2 Decision Tree Analysis

A **decision tree** is a graphical tool that maps out decisions and the uncertain outcomes that follow them. It contains two kinds of nodes:
- **Decision nodes** (squares): the manager chooses which branch to follow.
- **Information nodes** (circles): uncertainty is resolved exogenously.

A **real option** in a decision tree is a decision node that occurs *after* an information node — the manager can condition the choice on newly revealed information.

**Solving decision trees:** Work backward from the terminal date. At each decision node, select the branch with the highest present value. At each information node, compute the expected present value across branches.

**Worked Example (United Studios):**
- Producing both films simultaneously: expected payoff $= \$650\text{M} - \$525\text{M} = \$125\text{M}$.
- Producing sequentially (real option): if blockbuster (50% probability), produce sequel; if moderate hit (50%), do not.
  - Blockbuster payoff: $500 - 300 + 400 - 300 = \$300\text{M}$
  - Moderate-hit payoff (no sequel): $\$300\text{M}$
  - Expected: $-300 + 0.5(500 - 300 + 400) + 0.5(300) = \$150\text{M}$

The option to wait and decide is worth $\$150\text{M} - \$125\text{M} = \$25\text{M}$.

---

## 22.3 The Option to Delay: Investment as a Call Option

The option to **delay** an irreversible investment is equivalent to a call option: you pay the investment cost $I$ (the strike price) to acquire the project's value $V$ (the stock price). You exercise only when $V > I$.

### Valuation using Black-Scholes

| Financial option input | Real option analogue |
|---|---|
| Stock price $S$ | Current value of the operating asset $V$ |
| Strike price $K$ | Required investment $I$ |
| Time to expiration $T$ | Length of the investment window |
| Risk-free rate $r_f$ | Risk-free rate |
| Volatility $\sigma$ | Volatility of the asset value |
| Dividend | Free cash flows (value) lost by waiting |

The value of waiting is computed with $S^* = V - PV(FCF_{\text{lost}})$ and $PV(K) = I/(1+r_f)^T$.

**Worked Example (Electric car dealership):** $V = \$6\text{M}$, $I = \$5\text{M}$, $T = 1$ year, $\sigma = 40\%$, $r_f = 5\%$. First-year free cash flow $= \$0.6\text{M}$ (lost by waiting).

$$S^* = 6 - \frac{0.6}{1.12} = \$5.46\text{M}, \qquad PV(K) = \frac{5}{1.05} = \$4.76\text{M}$$

$$d_1 = \frac{\ln(5.46/4.76)}{0.40} + \frac{0.40}{2} = 0.543, \qquad d_2 = 0.543 - 0.40 = 0.143$$

$$C = 5.46 \times N(0.543) - 4.76 \times N(0.143) = 5.46(0.706) - 4.76(0.557) = \$1.20\text{M}$$

Since $\$1.20\text{M} > \$1.00\text{M}$ (NPV of investing today), **wait**.

### Key Factors

- **Higher volatility** ⟹ more value from waiting (more information to gain).
- **Higher "dividends" (lost cash flows)** ⟹ less value from waiting.
- **It is optimal to invest immediately only when NPV is substantially positive**, not just positive — the option to wait commands a premium.
- The beta of the option to invest is **higher** than the beta of the underlying project: $\beta_{\text{option}} = (S^*N(d_1)/C)\,\beta_{\text{project}}$.

---

## 22.4 Growth and Abandonment Options

### Growth Options

By undertaking a project today, a firm often acquires the right to expand in the future — a **growth option** (a real call option on future investments). Growth options are typically out-of-the-money and therefore riskier than existing assets, contributing to higher betas and expected returns for growth-oriented or R&D-intensive firms.

### The Option to Expand

When a project can be doubled in scale at a later date (at the original investment cost), the NPV of the total opportunity includes the value of the expansion call. Undertaking a small-scale pilot project may have negative standalone NPV but creates the expansion option, making the total NPV positive.

**Worked Example (business machines):** $I = \$10\text{M}$; risk-neutral probability of success $= 50\%$; perpetual cash flows $= \$1\text{M}$/yr if successful, $r_f = 6\%$.

- NPV without growth option: $\frac{0.5 \times 1}{0.06} - 10 = -\$1.667\text{M}$ (negative, don't invest).
- NPV of doubling if successful: $\frac{1}{0.06} - 10 = \$6.667\text{M}$.
- PV of growth option: $\frac{0.5 \times 6.667}{1.06} = \$3.145\text{M}$.
- **Total NPV** $= -1.667 + 3.145 = \$1.478\text{M} > 0$ ⟹ invest.

### Abandonment Options

An **abandonment option** is the right to stop a project and salvage its residual value. It is equivalent to a put option. When outcomes are uncertain, the ability to exit a losing project limits downside and can make an otherwise negative-NPV project worthwhile.

**Worked Example (Ferry Building store):** Setup cost \$400,000; monthly operating cost \$10,000; revenues \$8,000 (moderate) or \$16,000 (tourist attraction) with equal probability; option to break lease at no cost after 2 years; cost of capital 7%/yr.

- If tourist attraction: NPV $= \frac{16{,}000-10{,}000}{0.00565} - 400{,}000 = \$661{,}947$.
- If not: NPV $= \frac{(8{,}000-10{,}000)}{0.00565}\left(1-\frac{1}{1.00565^{24}}\right) - 400{,}000 = -\$444{,}770$ (close after 2 yrs).
- Expected NPV with abandonment: $0.5(661{,}947) + 0.5(-444{,}770) = \$108{,}589 > 0$.
- Option value: $108{,}589 - (-46{,}018) = \$154{,}607$.

**Key lesson:** Never dismiss an abandonment option as unimportant. Killing a failing project can create as much value as launching a new one. Managers must resist the sunk-cost fallacy.

---

## 22.5 Investments with Different Lives

When comparing mutually exclusive projects of different lengths, the shorter-lived project includes a **replacement option** at the end of its life. Simply comparing standalone NPVs ignores this option.

### Equivalent Annual Benefit (EAB) Method

The **Equivalent Annual Benefit** of a project converts its NPV into a constant annuity over the project's life:

$$\text{EAB} = \frac{\text{NPV}}{\text{Annuity factor}(r, T)}$$

Choosing the project with the higher EAB is equivalent to assuming the shorter-lived project can be **replaced at identical terms indefinitely**. This may be a reasonable assumption in stable markets but ignores real-world uncertainty about future costs and technology.

**Worked Example (Canadian Motors):** Five-year machine (NPV \$1.37M); ten-year machine (NPV \$2.43M); cost of capital 10%.

$$\text{EAB}_{5} = \frac{1.37}{3.791} = \$0.361\text{M}, \qquad \text{EAB}_{10} = \frac{2.43}{6.145} = \$0.395\text{M}$$

EAB favours the 10-year machine. However, if replacement costs are uncertain — say, equally likely to rise to \$13M, stay at \$10M, or fall to \$7M — then the 5-year machine's NPV over the 10-year horizon is:

$$NPV_{\text{5yr}} = 1.37 + \frac{\frac{1}{3}(0) + \frac{1}{3}(1.37) + \frac{1}{3}(4.37)}{1.10^5} = \$2.56\text{M}$$

Now the 5-year machine wins — the replacement option's value flips the decision.

---

## 22.6 Optimally Staging Investments

When a firm must invest in multiple dependent technologies (all must succeed for any payoff), the **order** of investment matters: undertake the project that resolves the most uncertainty per dollar invested first.

**Definition (Failure Cost Index):** For each stage, compute

$$\text{Failure Cost Index} = \frac{1 - PV(\text{success})}{PV(\text{investment})} \tag{22.3}$$

where $PV(\text{success})$ is the present value (at that stage's start) of receiving \$1 if the stage succeeds. **Rank stages from highest to lowest failure cost index and invest in that order.**

**Intuition:** Higher index ⟹ more uncertainty resolved per dollar spent ⟹ you learn more quickly whether to continue, minimising wasted investment.

General principles:
- Invest in **less costly** projects before more costly ones (avoid expensive investments that may prove unnecessary).
- Invest in **riskier / longer** projects first (learn the most before committing large sums).

---

## 22.7 Rules of Thumb

Exact real-option valuation is computationally demanding and problem-specific. Managers often use tractable approximations.

### Profitability Index Rule

Invest when the **profitability index** exceeds a threshold (commonly 1, rather than 0):

$$\text{PI} = \frac{\text{NPV}}{\text{Initial investment}} > \text{threshold}$$

Using PI $> 1$ (rather than the standard $> 0$) approximates the requirement that NPV be *substantially* positive before exercising the option to invest — it accounts for cash-flow uncertainty.

### Hurdle Rate Rule

Instead of raising the NPV bar, raise the **discount rate** used to compute NPV. Invest when NPV computed at the hurdle rate is positive:

$$\text{Hurdle rate} = r_{\text{cost of capital}} \times \frac{\text{Callable annuity rate}}{r_f} \tag{22.4}$$

Because callable loans let borrowers refinance if rates fall, they embed a prepayment option analogous to the firm's option to delay. The hurdle rate rule accounts for **interest-rate uncertainty**. In practice, a hurdle rate approximately 20% higher than the cost of capital (e.g. 12% instead of 10%) is a common approximation.

**Key lesson from real options:**
- Out-of-the-money real options have *positive* value.
- In-the-money real options need not be exercised immediately.
- Waiting is valuable — commit capital only when uncertainty is resolved.
- Tackle the most uncertain (highest failure cost index) problem first.
- Stage investments to maximise information gained per dollar spent.

key_concepts.explanation: A financial contract giving its holder the **right to buy** the underlying asset at the strike price $K$ on or before the expiration date. The payoff at expiration is $C = \max(S-K, 0)$. The holder is never obligated to exercise; the seller (writer) must fulfil the contract if exercised.

A financial contract giving its holder the **right to sell** the underlying asset at the strike price $K$ on or before the expiration date. Payoff at expiration: $P = \max(K-S, 0)$. Useful as portfolio insurance or a hedge against declining asset prices.

- **American option**: may be exercised on *any* date up to and including the expiration date. Worth at least as much as its European counterpart.
- **European option**: may be exercised *only* on the expiration date. Easier to price analytically.

The names have nothing to do with geography — both types trade worldwide.

For European options on a non-dividend-paying stock, the Law of One Price requires:
$$C = P + S - PV(K)$$
With dividends: $C = P + S - PV(Div) - PV(K)$. Violation implies an arbitrage opportunity. It allows any one of the four quantities ($C$, $P$, $S$, $PV(K)$) to be inferred from the other three.

**Intrinsic value** is the payoff from exercising immediately — the amount the option is in-the-money, or zero. **Time value** is the difference between the current option price and its intrinsic value. American options always have non-negative time value; European puts can have negative time value when deeply in-the-money.

Values an option by constructing a **replicating portfolio** of $\Delta$ shares and $B$ in risk-free bonds that matches the option payoff in every state. By the Law of One Price, $C = S\Delta + B$. The delta and bond position are:
$$\Delta = \frac{C_u - C_d}{S_u - S_d}, \qquad B = \frac{C_d - S_d\Delta}{1+r_f}$$
No knowledge of actual probabilities is required.

Closed-form price of a European call on a non-dividend-paying stock:
$$C = S \cdot N(d_1) - PV(K) \cdot N(d_2)$$
where $d_1 = \frac{\ln[S/PV(K)]}{\sigma\sqrt{T}} + \frac{\sigma\sqrt{T}}{2}$ and $d_2 = d_1 - \sigma\sqrt{T}$. Requires only five inputs: $S$, $K$, $T$, $r_f$, $\sigma$. The expected return on the stock is not needed.

The value of $\sigma$ that makes the Black-Scholes formula reproduce the *market price* of a traded option. Because all other inputs are observable, implied volatility is the market's consensus forecast of future stock volatility. The CBOE VIX index tracks one-month implied volatility of S&P 500 options.

Hypothetical probabilities $p$ and $(1-p)$ under which every asset's expected return equals the risk-free rate. In a binomial tree:
$$p = \frac{(1+r_f)S - S_d}{S_u - S_d}$$
Any derivative security can be priced by discounting its *risk-neutral* expected payoff at $r_f$. They are not the actual probabilities of each state.

$\Delta$ is the number of shares in the Black-Scholes replicating portfolio; equivalently, it is the sensitivity of the option price to a \$1 change in the stock price. For a call, $\Delta = N(d_1) \in (0,1)$; for a put, $\Delta = -(1-N(d_1)) \in (-1,0)$. As the option goes deeper in-the-money, $|\Delta|$ approaches 1.

In a levered firm with debt face value $D$:
- **Equity** = call option on firm assets $A$, strike $= D$. Payoff: $\max(A-D,0)$.
- **Risky debt** = risk-free debt minus a put on firm assets, strike $= D$. The put component is the *credit default swap*.

This view explains the asset-substitution problem (equity benefits from higher volatility) and debt overhang (equity captures only $\Delta < 1$ of each dollar of new investment).

The right — but not the obligation — to make a future business decision (invest, expand, abandon, delay) after new information is learned. Analogous to a financial call or put option, but the underlying asset is a business project rather than a traded security. Real options add value whenever there is uncertainty and managerial flexibility.

Used to rank the optimal order of mutually dependent staged investments:
$$\text{Failure Cost Index} = \frac{1 - PV(\text{success})}{PV(\text{investment})}$$
Stages are undertaken from **highest to lowest** index — resolving the most uncertainty per dollar first minimises the expected cost of a failed project.

common_mistakes: Confusing the *payoff* of an option with its *profit*. The payoff $\max(S-K,0)$ ignores the premium paid; profit subtracts the future value of the premium. An option can have a positive payoff but negative profit if the stock moved only slightly above the strike.

Applying put–call parity $C = P + S - PV(K)$ to American options. The formula is derived from the equivalence of two European-option portfolios; for American options it gives only a bound, not an equality.

Forgetting to subtract $PV(Div)$ from the stock price when applying put–call parity or Black-Scholes to dividend-paying stocks. Omitting dividends overstates both call prices and the replicating portfolio.

Treating the risk-neutral probability $p$ as the actual (physical) probability of an up move. Risk-neutral probabilities are a computational device; they equal actual probabilities only in a risk-neutral world. Using $p$ to compute expected returns gives the risk-free rate, not the stock's true expected return.

Concluding that an American call on a non-dividend-paying stock should sometimes be exercised early. It is *never* optimal: the time value (interest savings on the strike + put value) always exceeds the intrinsic value, so selling the call dominates early exercise.

Ignoring the option to delay when applying the NPV rule. The standard rule 'invest whenever NPV $> 0$' is only correct when there is no flexibility about timing. When delay is possible, invest only when NPV exceeds the value of waiting, which requires NPV to be substantially above zero.

Ranking mutually dependent staged investments by standalone NPV or cost alone. The correct ranking uses the **failure cost index** — smaller or riskier investments that resolve more uncertainty per dollar should come first, regardless of their standalone NPV.

Using the Black-Scholes formula directly to value employee stock options (ESOs). ESOs cannot be sold or hedged by the employee, are subject to vesting periods, and are typically exercised early. Black-Scholes systematically overstates ESO value for the employee and overstates the cost to the firm by ignoring forfeitures and early exercise.

Discounting risk-neutral expected payoffs at anything other than the risk-free rate. The entire point of risk-neutral pricing is that, under those probabilities, all assets earn $r_f$. Using a risk-adjusted rate double-counts the risk adjustment.
